topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the sterile finality of a death sentence. Dr. Albright was departing. I stared at the screen, the fluorescent light above my desk buzzing like an angry insect. It wasn't just a departure; it was the dismantling of the scaffolding that had held my entire academic structure up. I picked up my pen, the plastic cool against my fingertips, and found myself tracing the outline of the next chapter I’d painstakingly crafted. A tight knot formed in my stomach, a familiar, unpleasant clenching that made my jaw ache. I slammed the laptop shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet library annex. I walked to the window, watching the manicured quad, feeling a profound, heavy inertia settle over me. It felt like someone had just taken the main support beam out of my entire project.

[story 2]

"So, he's gone," Sarah mumbled, stirring her lukewarm coffee across the table. Her voice was flat, devoid of the usual warmth she reserved for discussing research. I didn't look up from the textbook open on my lap. Every word felt like an unnecessary imposition. I felt a distinct tightening behind my eyes, a slow burn starting low in my chest. When I finally spoke, my tone was clipped, too sharp. "Right. Well, that changes things." I pushed my chair back, the scrape of the legs against the linoleum sounding aggressive. I needed to reorganize everything, to re-verify every citation, not out of diligence, but because the uncertainty was starting to feel physically nauseating. I began pacing the small space between the bookshelves, my hands flexing involuntarily at my sides.

[story 3]

I reread the announcement three times. Each time, the same dull ache resonated in my molars. Dr. Albright, the one person who had somehow managed to translate my vague ideas into something tangible, a genuine intellectual partnership, was leaving. I gripped the edge of my desk until my knuckles were white, the pressure a physical manifestation of the churning dissatisfaction inside me. I couldn't shake the feeling that this was an unfair subtraction, a deliberate removal of the only anchor I had in this overwhelming academic sea. I started typing furiously, deleting entire paragraphs, the rhythm of my keystrokes erratic and jerky. When I finally stopped, I leaned back, letting out a slow, ragged breath that tasted faintly of stale coffee and frustration.
